name: Everlasting Yarrow Bundles
teaser: Flat papery flower heads that keep their shape and color hung to dry for weeks.
hook: Yarrow is a classic everlasting. Its flat clusters of tiny flowers hold their form and color for months once air dried, which is why bunches have long been hung to dry and worked into wreaths, swags, and dried bouquets. Here everlasting carries only its ordinary floristry sense, dried flowers that keep their shape, and the whole project is simply a matter of cutting, tying, and hanging. This card presents it strictly as a decorative drying and arranging craft.

timeline.active: 20 to 30 minutes to cut and tie
timeline.passive: 5 to 10 days to dry in dry conditions, up to 2 to 3 weeks in humid ones

yield: 2 to 3 small bundles of 8 to 12 stems each

ingredients.item: Yarrow flowering stems (Achillea millefolium), flat clusters in full open bloom
ingredients.note: Cut in the morning once flower heads are fully open and papery firm. Prefer garden grown, home patch, or florist stems over wild ones.
Rubber bands or elastics also work well and grip better as stems shrink.

toolsOptional: Clothespins, S hooks, or a drying rack
A dark, dry, well ventilated room or closet
steps: Once you have confirmed the plant is true yarrow (see safety), put on gloves and cut flowering stems in the morning after the dew has dried, choosing heads that are fully open and papery firm rather than soft and freshly opened. Take only a light share of any stand.
Strip the lower leaves from each stem so the bottom few inches are bare. This improves air flow inside the bundle and gives the twine clean stems to grip.
Gather 8 to 12 stems into a small bundle, keeping the flower heads at slightly staggered heights so air can move between them.

Wrap the bare cut ends snugly with twine and tie tight, or slip on a rubber band. The stems shrink as they dry, so bind them firmly and plan to re tighten twine after a day or two, or use an elastic that keeps its grip.

Hang each bundle upside down in a dark, dry, well ventilated spot, spacing bundles so air circulates around and through them. Darkness preserves the yellow and white color far better than a bright window.
Leave them for about 5 to 10 days in dry conditions, longer in humid weather, checking every few days for good air flow and no clumping.
The bundles are ready when the flower heads feel papery and the stems snap crisply instead of bending. Damp or bendy stems need more time.
Work the finished everlasting stems into wreaths, swags, and dried arrangements, and display them out of reach of pets and children.
modifiers: Yarrow comes in white, soft yellow, and warm garden shades of pink, coral, and red. Sort each bundle by color for a clean look, or mix shades for a meadow feel. Cutting longer stems gives you more to work with in tall arrangements. Golden flowered Achillea filipendulina holds an especially strong yellow and reads well in mixed everlasting bunches.
preservation: Dried yarrow keeps its shape and color for many months, often a year or more, when kept out of direct sun, damp, and heavy dust. Store spare stems flat or upright in a dry box. Faded or dusty heads can be swapped out as arrangements age.
safety: Identify your plant with certainty before cutting. True yarrow grows about 2 to 3 ft tall with soft, finely divided, feathery, fern like leaves and a fuzzy green stem with no purple markings, and it smells sweetly herbaceous. Do not cut white flat topped clusters from tall (5 to 10 ft) plants with smooth, hairless stems bearing purple spots or blotches, which is poison hemlock (Conium maculatum) and deadly, or from plants growing in wet ground with a chambered stem base, which is water hemlock (Cicuta species) and deadly. Both can be fatal, and poison hemlock toxins can pass through skin. If you are not certain, do not cut.

Prefer cultivated or purchased yarrow. For this purely decorative craft, garden grown Achillea, stems from your own patch, or florist and dried goods stems remove the field misidentification hazard almost entirely.

The finished dried bundle is a toxic household object. The ASPCA lists yarrow as toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, with vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, and skin irritation if chewed. Hang or display arrangements out of reach of pets and small children, and sweep up any dropped flower heads and stem fragments.
Wear gloves while cutting and stripping leaves, especially if you are ragweed sensitive, since handling yarrow foliage can cause an Asteraceae family skin reaction. Wash your hands after handling.

Watch for co occurring carrot family plants such as wild parsnip, wild carrot, and giant hogweed, whose sap can cause blistering skin burns on skin later exposed to sun. Cover your skin, wash your hands after cutting, and keep sap off bare skin.
This is a decorative craft only. Do not burn, smudge, steam, brew, or otherwise use the dried bundles on or in the body.
Occurrence on the map is not harvest permission. Take only a light share of any stand, and keep wild collected stems away from children and pets until identity is fully confirmed.

hook: Witch hazel (Hamamelis) is one of the very few North American shrubs that flowers in the dark half of the year, sending out spidery, ribbon-petaled yellow blooms along bare or nearly bare stems. The native common witch hazel (Hamamelis virginiana) flowers in fall to early winter, so its branches are simply cut while already in bloom for a vase. True forcing of tight dormant buds is a trick for the late winter hybrids (Hamamelis x intermedia), whose stems can be cut in midwinter and coaxed open indoors. Either way, cut branches have long been a favorite way to bring a little yellow and a light citrusy scent into a home during the gray months.

ingredients.item: Cut witch hazel stems (garden or nursery)
ingredients.note: From your own cultivated garden shrub, a shrub cut with the owner's permission, or nursery bought cut stems only. Cut just above a bud.

ingredients.amount: enough to fill the vase halfway
ingredients.note: Room temperature to warm water helps the stems drink; refresh every 2 to 3 days.
A common cut-branch trick to help the water stay fresh; plain water works too.

modifiers: For a midwinter version, use the late winter hybrids (Hamamelis x intermedia) in yellow, orange, or red: cut stems with tight dormant buds and let them truly force open indoors over a week or two. To build out an arrangement, stand a witch hazel stem with bare red twig dogwood or a few evergreen sprigs in the same vase.
name: Witch Hazel Cut Branches for a Winter Vase
preservation: A cool room, out of direct heat and sun, plus fresh water every 2 to 3 days keeps the blooms open longest, often 1 to 2 weeks. This is a decorative arrangement only: when it is spent, compost the stems. Do not dry, save, or process any part for skin or body use.
safety: This is a decorative cut-branch craft only. Do not steep, distill, dab, or apply the stems, leaves, or bark to skin, and do not ingest any part.

Keep the vase water out of reach of children and pets, and do not let anyone drink it, since swallowed plant tannins in the water can cause stomach upset. The foliage itself is listed as non-toxic to cats and dogs by the ASPCA, so the concern is the standing water rather than the plant.

Cut only from your own cultivated garden shrub, a shrub cut with the owner's explicit permission, or nursery bought cut stems. Never cut from wild or mapped plants, because the catalog ethic for witch hazel is observe only.
The occurrence map shows where a plant grows, never permission to harvest it.
Use standard care with sharp pruning tools. Cut just above a bud and take only a stem or two so you guide rather than harm the shrub's shape.
Identify your plant before cutting. Witch hazel (Hamamelis) shows spidery, ribbon-petaled yellow flowers along bare or nearly bare stems in fall through late winter, with wavy edged oval leaves.
External, non-body use only: this arrangement is for looking at, not for touching to skin or taking internally.

sources.title: Brooklyn Botanic Garden: Witch-Hazels: Winter Flowers, Fall Foliage, and Lovely Form
Iowa State University Yard and Garden: All About Witch Hazels
American Beauties Native Plants: Bring Spring Inside: Forcing Witchhazel to Bloom
UNH Extension: Which Witchhazel

steps: Source your stems first. Cut only from your own cultivated garden witch hazel, a shrub cut with the owner's explicit permission, or buy nursery cut stems. Take only a stem or two and cut just above a bud to guide the shrub's shape.

Match the plant to the season. For the native common witch hazel (Hamamelis virginiana), which flowers in fall to early winter, cut stems that already show open or opening blooms. For the late winter hybrids (Hamamelis x intermedia), you can cut stems with tight dormant buds in midwinter and force them.
Back inside, recut the base at an angle and split the bottom inch of each stem with your pruners or a light tap of a hammer so the woody stem can take up water.
Stand the stems in a vase filled halfway with cool to warm water, and set it in a bright, cool room away from direct heat and drafts.
If you like, add 1 tablespoon of clear lemon-lime soda per quart of water to help the water stay fresh; plain water is fine too.
Over several days the ribbon-like petals unfurl and lightly scent the room. If a warm then cold swing curls them shut, they reopen once it warms again.
Every 2 to 3 days, pour out the old water, refresh it, and recut the base about an inch to keep the stems drinking.
Keep the vase where children and pets cannot reach the water, and do not let anyone drink it. When the stems are spent, compost them.
teaser: Cut just a stem or two from your own garden shrub or from nursery stems: spidery yellow blooms and a light citrusy scent for a cool, bright winter room.

timeline.active: About 10 minutes to source, recut, and set up, plus a couple of minutes every 2 to 3 days to refresh the water.
timeline.passive: Several days for the petals to open indoors, with the arrangement lasting about 1 to 2 weeks.

toolsOptional: Hammer or small mallet (to split the woody stem base)
yield: One vase arrangement from a stem or two.

name: Witch Hazel and Rose Facial Splash
teaser: Dried rose and calendula slowly tint a jar of store bought witch hazel water to a pale amber.
hook: Commercial producers have distilled witch hazel twigs and bark into a clear scented water for well over a century, and barbers once kept a bottle on the shelf to dab on the skin after a shave. This project does not distill anything: you start from that finished, store bought witch hazel water and infuse it with dried rose petals and calendula until it takes on a soft color. It is a traditional grooming and skin care craft, made at home in a small jar. Witch hazel is observe only on this map, so no part of the wild shrub is collected.

timeline.active: About 15 minutes of hands on work
timeline.passive: 3 to 7 days steeping, plus a 24 hour patch test before first use

yield: About 1 cup (240 ml)

ingredients.note: Store bought pharmacy distillate, the kind sold for grooming. No wild witch hazel is harvested for this project.
Culinary or organically grown petals, not florist roses.
Optional. Leave out if you react to daisy family plants (see safety).

toolsRequired: Clean half pint (240 ml) glass jar with a tight lid
Bowl for a warm water bath
Coffee filter or fine cloth for straining
steps: Start from a fresh, sealed bottle of commercial witch hazel water, the distillate sold in pharmacies. Do not use any wild plant material: no witch hazel is harvested for this craft.
Measure about 1/2 cup of dried rose petals and 2 tablespoons of dried calendula petals into the clean, dry glass jar. If you are leaving the calendula out, use a little more rose.

Stand your measured witch hazel water (about 1 cup) in a bowl of hot tap water for a few minutes to take the chill off. Keep it well away from any open flame or hot burner, and never let it boil, because it contains alcohol and is flammable.
Pour the warmed witch hazel water over the petals until they are fully covered and the jar is nearly full. Cap it tightly and swirl so every petal is wet.
Set the jar in a cool spot out of direct light and let it steep 3 to 7 days, swirling once a day, until the liquid takes on color.
Strain through a coffee filter or fine cloth into a clean bottle, pressing gently to catch the last of the liquid, and compost the spent petals.
Label the bottle External Use Only with the date, and keep it in the refrigerator between uses.
Before the first use, dab a little on the inner forearm and wait 24 hours. If it stays clear, apply only to intact skin, at most once a day, and stop at any redness, stinging, or dryness.
modifiers: Leave the calendula out for a rose only version, which is the safer choice if you react to daisy family plants. Dried lavender buds or a short strip of dried organic orange peel can stand in for part of the rose. For a lighter feel on the skin, some makers cut the finished water with an equal part of plain rose hydrosol. Every version still contains alcohol and stays external use only.
preservation: Store in the refrigerator and use within about 2 weeks for the best color and freshness. The witch hazel water's own alcohol helps keep it, but the added botanicals shorten its life, so make small batches you can finish while fresh. Discard it if it turns cloudy, smells off, or grows any film.
safety: External use only. Do not swallow this, and keep it away from the eyes, mouth, and any broken or freshly shaved skin. Swallowed witch hazel water can upset the stomach.
Label the finished bottle External Use Only and store it out of reach of children and pets.
Commercial witch hazel water contains alcohol and is flammable. Warm it gently in a warm water bath, away from any open flame or hot burner, and never boil it.

Asteraceae (daisy family) allergy caution: calendula is a documented contact allergen. If you react to ragweed, daisies, chrysanthemums, or marigolds, or have related hay fever, leave the calendula out or skip this project.

Patch test on the inner forearm first, but know that a clear forearm test can still miss a reaction on the more delicate skin of the face. Apply only to intact skin, not right after shaving, no more than once a day, and stop at any redness, stinging, or dryness. People with rosacea or perioral dermatitis should skip it.
Use culinary or organically grown rose petals and calendula, not florist flowers, which can carry pesticide residues that are not meant for contact with skin.

No wild witch hazel is harvested for this project. Witch hazel is observe only on this map, and commercial distillate is made largely from the bark and wood, the restricted parts that need land manager permission on public land. Start only from finished, store bought witch hazel water. Occurrence points on the map are not permission to collect.
Identify your plant material: buy rose petals, calendula, and witch hazel water that are clearly labeled by a reputable supplier, so you know exactly what is going into the jar.

name: Cultivated Witch Hazel Twig Skin Wash
teaser: Garden prunings simmered down to an amber twig water, strained and jarred for external use only.
hook: Boiling witch hazel twigs and cooling the liquid is the old North American method behind the familiar witch hazel water. Early New England settlers learned the technique from Indigenous peoples, who prepared decoctions from the stems of this native shrub, and by the nineteenth century a bottled version was a common household item. This card presents the craft only: a way to reuse prunings from a cultivated garden witch hazel by simmering, cooling, and straining them into a small labeled batch. It describes technique and history and makes no claim about any effect on skin or body.

timeline.active: About 40 minutes: rinse and chop twigs, simmer 20 to 30 minutes, then strain.
timeline.passive: Cool to room temperature, then refrigerate and use within about two days.

yield: About 1.5 cups of strained twig water; a single small batch to keep refrigerated and discard within two days.

ingredients.item: Twigs from a cultivated garden witch hazel (Hamamelis virginiana), already pruned, or bought cut stem trimmings
ingredients.amount: A small handful, about 1 to 2 tablespoons chopped
ingredients.note: Start with a commercial witch hazel product or use prunings from a cultivated shrub. Do not wild harvest. Bought cut stem trimmings are a secondary path so no beginner is left with wild cutting as the only route.
Use ample water so the brew stays modest rather than a strong reduction.

toolsRequired: Clean glass jar with a lid
toolsOptional: Clean cotton pads or a soft cloth for a compress
steps: Source safely first: buy a commercial witch hazel product, or use twigs already pruned from a cultivated garden shrub, or bought cut stem trimmings. Do not cut from wild or mapped plants.
Rinse the twigs, chop a small handful (about 1 to 2 tablespoons) into short pieces, and put them in the saucepan with 2 cups of water so the twigs stay covered.
Bring to a boil, then reduce the heat and simmer gently for 20 to 30 minutes, topping up with a little water if needed to keep the twigs submerged. Keep it a modest brew, not a strong reduction.
Remove from the heat and let the liquid cool completely to room temperature.
Strain through the fine sieve or cheesecloth into the clean glass jar and discard the spent twigs.
Label the jar External Use Only with the date, then refrigerate.
Patch test before wider use: dab a little on the inner forearm, wait 24 to 48 hours, and check for redness or itching.
If the patch test is clear, use within about two days as a topical wipe on ordinary intact skin, or soak a cloth for a cool (cold temperature) compress. Discard any leftover after two days.
modifiers: For a cool (cold temperature) compress, chill the strained liquid and soak a clean cloth in it before laying it on ordinary intact skin. Keep every batch small and dilute, since a strong reduction is more irritating and gives no craft advantage. This project describes technique and history only and makes no claim about any effect on skin or body.
preservation: This is a preservative free water decoction. Refrigerate it in a closed, labeled glass jar and discard within about two days. Do not leave it at room temperature, do not can it, and do not blend it into lotions or store bought products.
safety: External use only. Never swallow this decoction. It is tannin rich, and taken by mouth in quantity it can upset the gut and stress the liver.

This home twig decoction is not the same product as the distilled witch hazel water sold in stores. The store product is a tannin free steam distillate, while this home version is tannin rich and has a meaningfully higher chance of irritating skin.

Witch hazel tannins are documented skin sensitizers and can cause irritant or allergic contact dermatitis. Patch test on the inner forearm, wait 24 to 48 hours, and check for delayed redness or itching before any wider use.
Use on ordinary, intact skin only. Keep it away from the eyes, the mouth and other mucous membranes, and any broken, weeping, eczematous, or rashy skin.
Keep the brew modest: a small amount of twig in ample water rather than a strong reduction, since a heavily concentrated decoction is more irritating.
Do not use on infants, on young children's skin, or on pets. Keep the labeled container out of reach of children and pets.
This is a preservative free water decoction. Make only small batches, keep them refrigerated, and discard within a couple of days.

Use only twigs already pruned from a cultivated garden shrub, or bought cut stem trimmings. Do not wild harvest witch hazel. It is an observe only species vulnerable to overharvest and local loss, and the map grants no cutting permission.

Identify your plant before using any prunings: American witch hazel (Hamamelis virginiana) shows strap shaped yellow flowers on bare branches in fall. Confirm the shrub with a garden label or a knowledgeable grower, since ornamental Hamamelis hybrids and other shrubs can look similar out of bloom.
This project describes technique and history only and makes no claim about any effect on skin or body.

name: Dried Elderflower Fragrance Bundles
teaser: Cream colored flower clusters, tied in threes and hung head down until they dry papery and hold the honeyed scent of early summer.
hook: Elder has been cut in early summer and hung to dry in farmhouses and stillrooms for generations, prized for the honeyed scent its cream colored flower clusters give off as they dry. This card presents elderflower purely as a seasonal botanical craft: a hanging bundle or a bowl of crumbled potpourri that captures the smell of the season. Nothing here is eaten or put on skin. The whole craft rests on a single skill, cutting the right plant.

timeline.active: About 20 minutes to harvest, shake, and tie
timeline.passive: 1 to 2 weeks of hanging or screen drying

yield: 3 to 4 hanging bundles, or about 2 cups of crumbled dried florets

ingredients.item: Fresh elder flower clusters (cymes), fully open and sweet smelling
ingredients.note: Cut each with 4 to 6 inches of stem. Prefer cultivated or purchased flowers as your default source.
For binding bundles of 3 to 5 cluster stems and leaving a hanging loop.

ingredients.item: Dried, food safe botanicals for a mixed potpourri, such as rose petals or dried citrus peel
ingredients.note: Optional. Keep elder content low and keep all of it away from food and utensils.

toolsOptional: Mesh drying screen or cooling rack
steps: On a dry morning after the dew has lifted, choose 8 to 12 flower clusters that are fully open and sweet smelling. Take only a light share from any one shrub, leaving most of the flowers for pollinators and birds, and snip each cluster with about 4 to 6 inches of stem.
Hold each cluster head down and tap or shake it gently for a few seconds to send resident insects on their way. Do not wash the flowers, since water browns the florets and slows drying.
Trim away the lower green stem, keeping just enough to tie. Gather 3 to 5 cluster stems and bind them near the cut ends with a length of twine, leaving a small loop for hanging.

Hang the bundles head down in a warm, dark, airy spot at roughly 80 F or cooler, spaced so air moves freely around each head. Alternatively, lay single heads in one layer on a mesh screen or a sheet of parchment.
Leave them undisturbed for 1 to 2 weeks, until the florets feel papery and rustle and the stems snap rather than bend.
Display the finished bundles as a hanging piece, or gently rub the dried florets off the stems over a bowl to collect loose potpourri. Discard the stripped green stems.
For a sachet, spoon about 1/2 cup of crumbled florets into a cloth bag and tie it closed. Keep every part of the finished piece well away from food surfaces, dishes, and utensils.
modifiers: Blend the crumbled florets with other dried, food safe botanicals such as rose petals, lavender buds, or dried citrus peel for a mixed potpourri, keeping the elder content low and every piece away from food. For a longer lasting hanging display, tie the dried bundle with a ribbon and refresh it each season, since the honeyed scent softens over a few weeks and is strongest when freshly dried.
preservation: Store loose dried florets in a labeled paper bag or a paper lined tin in a cool, dark, dry place. Paper lets any last moisture escape, while a tightly sealed jar can trap it and invite mold. Kept dry, the florets hold their look for many months, though the honeyed scent fades over a few weeks. Compost spent material once the scent is gone, keeping it away from pets.
safety: Identify the plant with certainty before you cut. Elder is a woody shrub or small tree with opposite, pinnately compound leaves and flat flower clusters (cymes) held over spongy white pith inside the stems.

Do not confuse elder with poison hemlock or water hemlock. Those are herbaceous plants with hollow or chambered green stems, finely divided alternate leaves, and umbrella shaped clusters, and they grow in and beside wet ditches. They are among the most lethal plants in North America.

Reject any white flowered plant on a soft green herbaceous stem, growing in or next to standing water, or smelling rank, musty, or of nothing. True elderflower smells sweet and honeyed. If you have any doubt, do not cut it.

Rule out toxic relatives in the same genus, including dwarf elder (Sambucus ebulus), a non woody plant whose flowers carry a ricin-like toxin, and red elderberry (Sambucus racemosa). Confirm the shrub with an expert or across seasons before harvesting.

Green stems and leaves are cyanogenic and toxic. Strip and discard as much green stem and leaf material as you can, keep only the dried florets, and keep all elder material out of anything that will later touch food or tea.

Hang or display the bundle and any potpourri out of reach of children and pets. Cats are especially sensitive to cyanogenic plant material, and signs can appear soon after they mouth it. Wash your hands after handling.
Occurrence and map data are not harvest permission and do not confirm a plant's identity. Verifying identity on the ground is the maker's responsibility.

Prefer cultivated or purchased elderflower as your default source. This decorative fragrance craft does not require wild harvest, and buying or growing the flowers removes the lethal lookalike risk for beginners.

name: Coneflower Seed Heads for the Winter Garden and the Birds
teaser: Stiff, spiky cones left standing to catch the frost and feed winter finches.
hook: Long before it was potted up as a garden border ornamental, purple coneflower stood through the open winter as a stiff, seed-filled cone. Gardeners have kept that habit on purpose: the browned cones give a bed its winter architecture and hold seed that goldfinches and sparrows work loose in the cold months. This card presents the plant purely as standing garden structure and bird food, and as seed you can save from cultivated plants and resow.

timeline.active: About 30 minutes to clip and clean a few cones
timeline.passive: About 2 weeks of drying; cones can stand in the bed all fall and winter

yield: A labeled envelope of dried coneflower seed, plus standing seed heads that hold their structure and feed birds through winter

ingredients.item: Cultivated purple coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) with spent, browned seed cones
ingredients.amount: A few plants, or 3 to 6 fully dry cones if saving seed
ingredients.note: Grown or bought as a garden plant. Above-ground spent tops and cones only, no roots.

ingredients.item: Paper envelope or small paper bag for storage
ingredients.note: Label it with the plant name and the year so seed does not get mixed up.

toolsRequired: A bowl to break the cones apart over
toolsOptional: Paper bag with a few small holes punched in it, for drying
Kitchen strainer or sieve for separating seed from chaff
Dust mask if you are sensitive to daisy-family pollen or dust
steps: Through late summer, stop deadheading your cultivated coneflower and let the last flush of flowers finish and set seed on the plant.

Leave the stiff, spiky central cones standing on their stems through fall and winter for garden structure and for seed-eating birds such as goldfinches and sparrows, which peck the seed straight from the dry cones.
To also save seed, pick a dry day and choose a few cones that have turned fully brown and hard. Put on garden gloves first, because the cones are prickly.
Clip those cones with a long stem and drop them head first into a paper bag with a few small holes punched in it. Hang the bag in a dry, well ventilated spot for about two weeks to finish drying.

Working outdoors or in a well ventilated spot, break the dry cones apart over a bowl and rub them between gloved fingers. Look for the tan, cone-shaped seeds in the center; the long, needle-like spikes are chaff, not seed.
Tip the seed and chaff into a kitchen strainer and toss it gently so the lighter chaff falls or blows away, leaving the heavier dark seed behind.
Let the seed air-dry fully, then pour it into a labeled paper envelope and keep it in a cool, dry place to sow next spring.
In late winter or early spring, cut the remaining standing stems back to tidy the bed. Native bees sometimes overwinter in the hollow stems, so waiting until then rewards them as well as the birds.
modifiers: If your only goal is winter interest, leave every cone in place and skip seed saving entirely: the frosted silhouette and the birds are the whole point. Note that named cultivars and hybrids often do not come true from saved seed, so seedlings grown from a purchased hybrid may vary in flower color and form.
preservation: Store fully dried seed in a labeled paper envelope in a cool, dry, dark place; paper lets any last moisture escape rather than trapping it. Keep the envelope away from damp so the seed does not clump or grow mold, and sow the following spring. Standing cones left on the plant need no storage; they hold through fall and winter and are cut back in late winter.
safety: Wear gloves when you clip and break apart the dried seed cones. The spiky central cones are prickly.

Coneflower is in the daisy (Asteraceae) family. If you are sensitive to ragweed or other daisy-family plants, break the dry cones apart outdoors or in a well ventilated spot so you are not breathing the airborne chaff and dust.
This is a garden and wildlife craft only. Do not eat or otherwise put any part of the plant to use, and do not prepare it for any human use.
Use only coneflower that you grow or buy as a cultivated garden plant. Occurrence points on the map are not permission to collect seed or plants from land you do not manage.
Collect no roots. Wild Echinacea roots are overharvested and some wild Echinacea species are rare or federally protected, so this craft uses only cultivated, above-ground parts.

Identify your plant before you save seed. Purple coneflower has pinkish-purple, downward-drooping ray petals and a raised, coppery, spiky central cone. Black-eyed Susan (Rudbeckia) looks similar but has yellow petals and a darker, smoother brown to near-black central dome, so keep the two apart when you collect and label seed.
This is not food. Keep the dry cones, the loose chaff, and the saved seed away from children and pets, and store the labeled envelope out of their reach.

name: Dried Coneflower Bundles and Wreaths
teaser: Stiff stems, papery ray petals, and a dark bristled cone that keeps its shape long after the season ends.
hook: Purple coneflower has been a favorite of cut flower gardeners for generations, prized as much for its stiff seed cone as for its ray petals. Cut and hung to dry, the blooms hold their form for arrangements, wreaths, and ornamental bunches, and the bare cones make a spiky dark accent once the petals fall. This project is decorative only and makes no reference to eating, brewing, or any traditional health use of the plant.

timeline.active: About 30 minutes to cut, strip, and bind
timeline.passive: 2 to 3 weeks hanging to dry

yield: 2 to 3 dried bundles, or one small wreath

ingredients.item: Coneflower flowering stems (Echinacea purpurea), cultivated only
ingredients.note: Cut from your own bed or nursery grown plants. Choose freshly open blooms on firm stems, ideally in the morning after dew dries. No roots and no bark.
Only needed if you are building a wreath rather than hanging loose bundles.
Optional finishing for a hung bundle or wreath.

toolsRequired: Sharp bypass pruners or floral snips
steps: Wearing gloves and long sleeves, cut in the morning once the dew has dried. Choose freshly open blooms on firm stems from your own bed or a nursery grown plant, and cut stems 12 to 18 inches long with clean pruners.
Strip the lower leaves by holding a stem just below the flower and running a gloved hand down toward the cut end. Leaves hold moisture and slow the dry, so remove all of the lower foliage.

Gather small bunches of about 8 to 12 stems, offsetting the heads so the cones do not crush each other. Bind the stem ends with a rubber band or twist tie rather than string, since the band keeps tension as the stems shrink.

Hang the bunches upside down from twine or hooks in a warm, dark, dry, well ventilated space. Darkness holds the petal color and good airflow prevents mold, so space the bundles apart so air moves around each one.
Leave the bundles undisturbed for 2 to 3 weeks, until the stems snap cleanly and the central cones feel hard and dry to the touch.

For a spiky textural accent, cut the cones alone once the petals have dropped and dry them the same way, or wire short dried stems in overlapping fan shaped tufts around a grapevine or wire frame to build a wreath.
Optional: a light pass of clear matte spray sealant helps hold the petals and reduce shatter. Keep finished pieces in a dry indoor spot out of direct sun.
modifiers: Cones only: wait until the petals have dropped, cut the bare cones, and dry them for a darker, spikier accent. Wreath: wire short dried stems in overlapping fan shaped tufts around a grapevine or wire frame. Color: coneflower petals fade toward tan over the months, so pair them in a mixed bundle with statice or goldenrod, whose blues and yellows hold color longest when air dried.
preservation: Fully dried bundles keep their form for a year or more in a dry indoor spot out of direct sun. Humidity softens the stems and invites mold, so avoid bathrooms and basements. A light coat of clear matte spray sealant reduces petal shatter. Store the pieces hanging or laid flat, and never box them while any moisture remains in the stems.
safety: This is a decorative botanical craft only. Nothing here is eaten, brewed, or applied to the body.

Identify your plant before cutting. Coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) has rough, coarsely toothed lance shaped leaves and a single daisy like flower with a raised, bristly central cone. Do not confuse a young coneflower rosette with foxglove (Digitalis), whose soft, downy, finely scalloped leaves form a low basal rosette and which is dangerous to handle and to keep near food. Foxglove never forms the raised bristly cone.

Coneflower is in the daisy and aster family (Asteraceae). People who are sensitive to ragweed, chamomile, feverfew, chrysanthemums, or daisies can get skin irritation from the sap, or a pollen reaction while cutting, stripping, and drying. Wear gloves and long sleeves if you are sensitive.
Dry the bundles in a well ventilated space rather than a small closed room. Good airflow limits pollen and dust buildup indoors during the multi week dry.

Use cultivated material only: home grown, garden bed, or nursery bought plants. Do not wild harvest coneflower. Some wild coneflowers are protected or declining (Echinacea laevigata is federally listed), and native prairie stands should be left to set seed.
Map occurrence data is not harvest permission and never marks a plant as free to cut.

Keep finished dried arrangements out of reach of children and of pets that might chew them, and away from food surfaces. Coneflower is listed as non toxic to pets, with only minor irritation possible at worst if a pet chews it.

name: Pressed Coneflower Botanical Art
teaser: Pink-purple coneflower petals flattened onto paper and kept behind glass.
hook: Pressing flowers is a botanical art tradition that runs from sixteenth century herbarium sheets to Victorian scrapbooks, preserving a bloom's exact form and color on paper. The purple coneflower presses well: its long ray petals and domed central cone hold their shape and their pink-purple color for years behind glass. This card treats the plant purely as a subject for framed art, bookmarks, and greeting cards. Presented strictly as pressed-flower art, with no reference to any medicinal or edible use.

timeline.active: 30 to 45 minutes of hands-on cutting, arranging, and later mounting
timeline.passive: 2 to 3 weeks pressing and drying undisturbed

yield: 6 to 12 pressed blooms and loose petals, enough for several bookmarks or cards or one framed piece

ingredients.item: Freshly opened coneflower blooms from a cultivated bed
ingredients.note: Cut on a dry morning after the dew has burned off, choosing firm, unblemished flowers.

ingredients.amount: 6 to 12 folded sheets per layer, above and below
ingredients.note: Blotting paper can be reused; newspaper is cheapest but swap it out as it dampens.

ingredients.item: Cardstock or heavy acid-free paper for mounting
ingredients.note: Acid-free stock keeps the mount from yellowing over time.

ingredients.item: Archival PVA glue or acid-free glue dots
ingredients.amount: a few small dots per bloom
ingredients.note: Apply only at the petal bases so the glue does not show.

ingredients.item: Clear self-adhesive film or a glass frame
ingredients.note: Seals the art against handling and humidity.

toolsRequired: Flower press or a heavy book
toolsOptional: Tweezers for lifting fragile pressed petals
Soft brush for dusting off pollen
steps: On a dry morning after the dew has burned off, put on gloves and cut 6 to 12 freshly opened coneflower blooms from your own cultivated bed, choosing flowers with firm, unblemished petals.

Work outdoors or beside an open window. For a flat press, either pull the ray petals free one at a time, or lay a bloom on the cutting board and slice it in half from top to bottom through the cone with a craft knife so it will lie flat.

Arrange the petals and halved blooms in a single layer, not touching, between two sheets of absorbent paper. Stack 6 to 12 folded sheets of newspaper or blotting paper above and below to soak up moisture.
Close the layers inside a flower press tightened evenly, or weight them under a heavy book, and set them somewhere dry and warm.
For the first few days, open the press and swap in fresh dry paper each day so the blooms do not brown, then change the paper every few days after that.
Leave the flowers undisturbed for two to three weeks until they are fully dry and papery to the touch, with no cool or damp feel at the cone.
Lift each pressed piece with tweezers, arrange it on cardstock, and fix it down with tiny dots of archival glue at the petal bases.
Seal the finished piece under a sheet of clear self-adhesive film, or set it behind glass in a frame, to protect it from handling and humidity.
modifiers: For bookmarks, mount a single petal or a slim half-bloom on a narrow card strip and laminate it. For greeting cards, glue a whole pressed bloom to folded cardstock. Pressing the halved cone shows the plant's structure, while pressing loose petals gives a lighter, more scattered design. Pot marigold, cosmos, and larkspur from the same cultivated bed press well alongside coneflower for mixed arrangements.
preservation: Keep finished pieces out of direct sunlight, which fades the pink and purple over months. Store loose pressed blooms flat between sheets of paper in a dry box until you mount them. Sealed under film or glass and kept dry, mounted pieces hold their color and shape for years.
safety: This is a decorative craft only. Nothing here is eaten or put on the skin, and it is not a medicine or a health product of any kind.

Identify your plant with confidence before cutting. Garden coneflower (Echinacea purpurea) has a raised, bristly orange-brown central cone with drooping pink to purple ray petals. Do not confuse it with black-eyed Susan (Rudbeckia hirta), which has a darker brown to near-black dome and yellow petals. Either way, cut only named plants from your own cultivated bed.

Coneflower is in the Asteraceae (daisy) family. Its fresh leaves, stems, flowers, and pollen contain compounds that can cause an itchy contact rash. People allergic to ragweed, chrysanthemums, daisies, or marigolds are especially likely to react, so wear gloves when cutting and pressing the blooms and keep your hands away from your eyes and face.
The allergy and pollen risk is present only during the fresh picking and pressing step. The finished, fully dried art under glass poses no such exposure.
Fresh pollen can set off hay fever or asthma symptoms in people who are sensitive to it. Work outdoors or in a well ventilated space and avoid breathing in pollen when you halve the blooms.

Use only flowers you grow yourself or buy from cultivated plants. Do not pick from wild stands or restoration plantings, because several wild coneflower species are under harvest pressure or federally listed. Use the flowers only, never the roots.
Keep cut plant material, gloves, and the finished art away from children and pets, since the blooms are for display only and are not food.
Occurrence points on the map show where a plant has been recorded. They are not permission to pick, and they never grant harvest rights on any land.

name: Hag's Taper: a dipped mullein garden torch
teaser: A dead winter stalk built up in beeswax until it stands like a fat candle.
hook: Long before wax candles were common, a dried mullein stalk dipped in fat made a cheap standing torch. The Romans dipped mullein stalks in tallow (rendered beef or mutton fat) and carried them in funeral processions, and the plant kept a whole shelf of torch-and-candle folk names down the centuries: Hag's Taper, Candlewick, King's Candle, Torches. This card revives that old outdoor craft with beeswax, which burns cleaner than tallow, using the plant's dead second-year seed stalk after it has finished its life and dried standing in the field. Presented purely as a botanical craft that reuses the plant's tall dead stalk, not for any use on the body.

timeline.active: About 1 hour to cut, strip, melt wax, and dip the stalk.
timeline.passive: 48 hours of cooling and hardening before the torch is lit.

yield: 1 garden torch, about 30 to 60 minutes of open-air burn (roughly 10 minutes per inch of waxed head).

ingredients.item: Dried second-year mullein flower stalk (Verbascum thapsus), standing dead seed stalk
ingredients.note: Cut only a bone-dry, fully dead stalk. The woolly pith of the seed-head end is what holds the wax.
Pellets melt faster. You want enough depth in the pot to submerge the seed-head end of the stalk.

ingredients.amount: Enough to fill the outer pot of the double boiler
ingredients.note: For the water bath only. Wax is never heated directly over the flame.
Optional. Pressed into the warm wax at the tip, it makes the torch easier to light.

toolsRequired: Tall double boiler, or a tall clean metal can set inside a pot of water
A closed bag or lidded container for the stripped seed capsules
Metal torch holder or a metal ground spike to stand the base in
toolsOptional: A second tall metal vessel deep enough to dip into
A comb or gloved hand for stripping seed heads
steps: Positively identify the plant, then cut. Confirm mullein by its single tall stalk topped with a dense woolly seed spike and, at the base, a felted fuzzy leaf rosette. Cut one bone-dry dead stalk near ground level with a saw or loppers. Do this outdoors, wearing gloves and eye protection, and avoid crushing the fuzzy stalk near your face.

Strip the seed heads outdoors. Working over a closed bag or lidded container, shake and comb the mature seed capsules off the spike so they cannot scatter. Seal the bag and put it in household trash. Keep the stripped seed away from children and pets.

Set up the water bath and melt the wax. Fill the outer pot with water, set the inner vessel of beeswax inside, and warm over medium heat until the wax is fully liquid, about 150 to 160 F. Keep the wax over the water bath the whole time, never directly over the flame.

Dip the seed-head end. Lower the woolly top of the stalk into the melted wax, hold it a few seconds so the pith soaks, then lift it out and let the coat set for one to two minutes until it clouds and firms.

Build up the head. Repeat the dip-and-cool cycle 10 to 20 times, letting each coat cool between dips, until a waxed shell about a quarter to a half inch thick has built up around the seed-head end. If using the optional wick, press it into the warm wax at the very tip during the last few dips.
Harden the torch. Stand or lay the finished torch in a cool, dry spot and let it set undisturbed for 48 hours before burning so the wax fully hardens.

Burn it outdoors only. Set the base in a metal holder or ground spike on bare soil, well away from dry brush, branches, and structures, with water within reach. Scrape a small bare spot at the very tip, light it, and stand upwind. Expect roughly 10 minutes of burn per inch of waxed head; the pith wicks fast and the head can drip hot wax.
modifiers: Traditional Roman torches used rendered tallow instead of beeswax. Tallow is the authentic material but it smokes more, so beeswax is the cleaner-burning choice here. More dips build a thicker shell that burns longer, while a slimmer shell lights and catches faster. Cutting the stalk shorter (12 to 18 inches) makes a tabletop-height garden torch that is easier to seat firmly in a holder.
preservation: Store the finished torch upright or flat in a cool, dry place out of direct sun and away from any heat source, so the wax does not soften or slump. Kept dry, a waxed stalk holds for years. Keep the stored torch and any leftover seed away from children and pets.
safety: Positively identify the plant before you cut. Confirm mullein by its single tall stalk topped with a dense woolly seed-capsule spike and a felted, fuzzy leaf rosette at the base. Other tall biennials leave similar dead stalks standing through winter. Poison hemlock (Conium maculatum) has a smooth, hairless, hollow stalk blotched and streaked with purple or maroon, never woolly, and it stays toxic when dead and gives off poisonous smoke when burned. Teasel leaves a rigid, prickly, egg-shaped spiny seed head, unlike mullein's soft felted spike. Never cut, dip, or burn a stalk you have not positively identified as mullein.

Strip the seed heads before you dip or burn. Shake or comb the mature seed capsules into a closed bag and put them in household trash, and never scatter them on the ground, which keeps this weedy seed from spreading and keeps you from burning the plant's most concentrated part. Mullein seeds contain rotenone and saponins, so keep them away from children and pets and never eat them.

Burn with open flame outdoors only. Use it in the open air, never indoors and never on an enclosed or screened porch. Keep the torch well away from dry brush, overhanging branches, and structures, set the base in a metal holder on bare soil, and keep water within reach. The pith wicks fast and the head can drip hot wax.

Stay out of the smoke. Stand upwind, do not lean over the flame or fan it, and keep people with breathing difficulties, pregnant people, children, and pets out of the smoke plume. Burning any plant material produces fine-particle smoke and combustion irritants.

Handle the dry stalk with care. Cut and process it outdoors, wear gloves and eye protection, avoid crushing the fuzzy stalk near your face, and wash your hands afterward. The dry, branched (stellate) leaf and stem hairs shed easily and can irritate skin, eyes, and airways.
Dispose of ash, burnt debris, and stripped seed in household trash, never near ponds, streams, or storm drains, because mullein saponins are toxic to fish.

Occurrence is never harvest permission. The map shows where mullein grows, not where you may gather it or light a flame. Cut dead plant material only where it is allowed, preferring your own garden, waste ground, or disturbed roadsides where mullein is abundant, and remember that many parks and preserves prohibit all collection and all open flame.
This is a garden craft object, not for any use on the body. Keep the finished torch and all materials away from children and pets.

name: Wild Bergamot Aromatic Bundles and Sachets
teaser: Square-stemmed prairie wildflower bound into bundles and dried for a lasting oregano-mint-citrus scent.
hook: Wild bergamot is a square-stemmed mint-family wildflower of North American prairies and meadows, topped with a shaggy lavender flower head that hums with bees in midsummer. Early gardeners and households prized its foliage for a lasting fragrance and kept dried bundles about the home. This card treats it purely as a scent and decoration craft: cut a light share of the flowering tops, dry them in an airy spot, and strip the brittle leaves into sachets or a bowl of potpourri.

timeline.active: 20 to 30 minutes to cut, bind, and hang, plus about 15 minutes later to strip and bag
timeline.passive: 5 to 10 days of hanging until fully dry

yield: One hanging bundle plus 2 to 4 small sachets, or a bowl of potpourri

ingredients.item: Wild bergamot flowering tops (top 6 inches of stem)
ingredients.note: Clean, mildew-free foliage only, cut cultivated, bought, or from a large abundant stand
For binding the cut ends, tied loose so air moves through the bundle

ingredients.item: Cotton muslin sachet bags or cloth squares
ingredients.note: Only needed if you plan to make sachets rather than a hanging bundle

ingredients.item: Other dried garden flowers such as calendula or lavender
ingredients.note: Optional, for mixing into potpourri

toolsRequired: A warm, dry, airy spot out of direct sun
steps: Identify the plant with certainty. Wild bergamot has a square stem, opposite aromatic leaves, and a single dense lavender flower head. Crush one leaf to confirm the oregano-mint scent. Cut only from a large, dense stand and take just a small share, or use plants you grow or buy.

Choose clean stems. Inspect the leaves for a white powdery coating or orange rust spots and skip any that show them. Using snips, cut the top 6 inches from 8 to 12 healthy flowering stems, leaving most of the tops standing for pollinators.

Strip the leaves from the lowest inch or two of each cut end. Gather the stems into a loose bundle and bind the bare ends together with twine. Keep the bundle loose, not packed tight, so air can move through it and the stems do not mold.
Hang the bundle upside down in a warm, dry, airy spot out of direct sun. Direct sun fades both the color and the scent, so a shaded corner, closet, or covered porch works well.
Leave the bundle 5 to 10 days. It is fully dry when the thickest stem snaps cleanly instead of bending and the leaves crumble between your fingers. Damp weather lengthens this time.

Use the finished bundle as-is as a fragrant hanging decoration, or strip the dried leaves and flower petals into muslin sachet bags or a bowl. The dried foliage keeps a lasting oregano-mint-citrus scent.

Work in a well-ventilated space when you strip or crush the brittle foliage. Store any spare dried leaves in a sealed jar away from light, and squeeze a sachet to bruise the leaves when its scent starts to fade.
modifiers: For potpourri, toss the stripped petals and leaves with other dried garden flowers such as calendula or lavender in a shallow bowl. For a longer-lasting hanging bundle, bind in a few dried grass seed heads or yarrow stalks alongside the bergamot for shape and texture. Keep spare dried leaves in a sealed jar away from light and refresh a tired sachet by squeezing it to bruise the leaves.
preservation: Fully dried and kept in a sealed jar away from light and heat, the stripped leaves and petals hold their scent for several months up to about a year. Sachets fade sooner with handling, so squeeze them to renew the scent and replace the contents once squeezing no longer revives it. Discard any batch that smells musty or shows fuzzy growth, which means moisture was trapped before drying finished.
safety: Identify your plant before cutting. Wild bergamot has a square stem, opposite aromatic leaves, and one dense lavender flower head. Confirm all three features before you harvest.

Harvest only clean, mildew-free foliage. Skip any leaves or stems with a white powdery coating or orange rust spots instead of drying them. Strip or crush the dried leaves in a well-ventilated space, since crushing diseased dried foliage can release fungal spores.
This is a scent and decoration craft only. Do not eat it, brew it, apply it to skin, or burn it indoors.

Keep hanging bundles and sachets out of reach of cats and other pets, and away from small children. The thymol and carvacrol in the dried foliage are phenolic compounds that cats process poorly, so chewing the material can cause vomiting or stomach upset.
Take only a small share of a large, dense stand and leave the flowering tops for pollinators. Growing or buying Monarda is the preferred, low-impact source for this craft.

Map occurrence shows only where the plant grows. It is not harvest permission. Many parks, preserves, and roadsides prohibit all plant collection regardless of abundance, so confirm the land manager allows harvest before cutting.

name: Wild Bergamot Leaf Tea, One of the Colonial Liberty Teas
teaser: A fragrant oregano, thyme, and citrus garden tisane.
hook: Wild bergamot was one of several native plants that colonists brewed as a homegrown tea substitute during the colonial tea boycotts of the 1770s, when imported black tea was set aside. Long before that, Indigenous peoples across its range steeped the leaves and flowering tops as a garden beverage. Its lavender pom-pom flowers and leaves carry a bright oregano-and-thyme scent that fills a mug with garden aroma. This card dries the summer cuttings and steeps them into a simple, fragrant tisane.
modifiers: Blend the dried bergamot with garden mint or lemon balm for a milder cup, or add a few of the dried lavender colored florets for color. For a cold version, steep the same measure in cool water in the refrigerator for several hours, then strain and serve over ice. Keep every version a normally brewed, single strength beverage rather than a concentrated brew. Closely related garden bee balms such as scarlet Monarda didyma can be dried and brewed the same way for a similar aromatic cup.
preservation: Store the dried leaves and flowers whole in a cool, dark, airtight jar and use within about a year, when the aroma is strongest. Keep the jar away from heat, light, and moisture, and check now and then for any musty smell or mold, discarding the batch if you find either. Freezing is unnecessary for a dried herb kept dry.
yield: A pint jar of dried herb from a summer's light cuttings, enough for dozens of cups. One heaping tablespoon brews a single mug.

timeline.active: About 15 minutes of hands on work, plus a few minutes to brew a cup
timeline.passive: 1 to 2 weeks to air dry, or a few hours in a dehydrator

ingredients.item: Wild bergamot leaves and flowering tops (Monarda fistulosa), picked in bloom
ingredients.note: Cut only clean foliage that is free of powdery mildew and rust spots. Leave most flower heads standing for pollinators.
Take the kettle off the boil before pouring.
Optional, for flavor only. The herb is fragrant enough to drink plain.

toolsRequired: Twine for bundles or a drying rack
A kettle and a mug or teapot with a strainer
toolsOptional: A dehydrator with a low herb setting
A fine mesh tea strainer or infuser
steps: Cut leaves and flowering tops while the plant is in full bloom, choosing only clean, dry foliage with no mildew or rust. Take mostly leaves and leave most flower heads in place as pollinator forage.
Give the cuttings a gentle rinse only if needed, then pat and shake them fully dry, since surface moisture invites mold during drying.

To air dry, gather small loose bundles, tie the stems, and hang them upside down in a warm, airy spot out of direct sun for 1 to 2 weeks until the leaves are brittle and crumble easily. To speed it up, lay them in a single layer in a dehydrator on a low herb setting around 100 to 130 F (38 to 54 C) until crisp.
Strip the dried leaves and petals from the stems, crumble them lightly, and store them whole leaf in a cool, dry, airtight jar away from light. Discard any batch that smells musty or shows mold.
To brew a cup, put about one heaping tablespoon of the crumbled dried herb in a mug or infuser and pour over one cup of just boiled water.
Cover the cup and steep for about 5 minutes, no longer, then strain out the herb. Do not simmer it or let it steep for a long time, as the brew turns harsh.
Taste before adding more herb. The flavor is assertive, so start light and adjust to taste, sweetening with a little honey or a lemon slice if you like.
safety: Do not use during pregnancy.
Avoid while breastfeeding as a precaution, since safety data during lactation is limited.

Enjoy this only as an occasional, normally brewed beverage. Do not make concentrated decoctions, do not over steep, and never use essential oil strength preparations. The leaf is rich in the aromatic compounds thymol and carvacrol, which can irritate the mouth, stomach, and mucous membranes when concentrated.
If you take prescription medication, check with a doctor or pharmacist before adding any new herbal beverage, since concentrated botanicals can interact with some drugs.

Identify your plant before picking, and confirm it only when it is in bloom, using the lavender pom pom flower head, square stem, opposite leaves, and strong oregano and thyme aroma together. Related garden bee balms in the same genus (Monarda) are used for tea the same way, so a mix up within the genus is low risk.
Cut only clean foliage that is free of powdery mildew and rust.

Prefer garden grown material or an established patch you own. Use wild collection only as a fallback, take mostly leaves, and leave the flowering tops in place as forage for pollinators, including specialist bees that rely on this plant.
Map occurrence is not harvest permission. Get the land manager's permission before collecting on public or protected land.
Keep the dried herb and the brewed tea out of reach of children and pets.
Educational and traditional use reference only. This is a beverage, not medical advice, and it carries no health claims and no dosing.

name: Wild Bergamot in the Kitchen: Petals and Pizza Plant Leaf
teaser: A native mint family plant with mild, faintly citrusy petals and a bold oregano-thyme leaf that cooks nickname the pizza plant.
hook: Wild bergamot, also called bee balm, is a native mint family plant of North American prairies and meadows with a long food and beverage record. Cooks have scattered its lavender florets over dishes as edible confetti and used its leaves the way a Mediterranean kitchen reaches for oregano or thyme. Crush a leaf and the aroma is unmistakable, which is how it earned the kitchen nickname pizza plant. This card presents it purely as a culinary flavoring and edible garnish.

timeline.active: About 20 minutes of hands on prep
timeline.passive: 1 to 2 weeks to air dry the leaves

yield: Fresh garnish plus roughly 1/2 cup of crumbled dried leaf from a few garden plants.

ingredients.item: Fresh wild bergamot leaves (Monarda fistulosa), from garden or nursery plants
ingredients.amount: 1 small handful, about 1/2 cup loosely packed
ingredients.note: Pick leafy tops just before or as the plant flowers, when the leaf is most tender and aromatic.
For pulling individual florets as garnish; take only a few from cultivated plants and leave the rest for pollinators.

ingredients.item: Dishes to flavor: tomato sauce, marinade, vinaigrette, soft cheese, salad, or a summer drink
ingredients.note: The leaf pairs well anywhere you would use a light touch of oregano or thyme.
Rinse and pat or spin the leaves and florets dry before use.

steps: Pick leafy tops and a few flower heads from your own garden or nursery plants, ideally just before or as they flower. Rinse gently in cool water and pat or spin dry.
For an edible garnish, pull the individual florets from a clean flower head and scatter about 1 tablespoon over a salad, a plate of soft cheese, or a summer drink as a mild, faintly citrusy confetti.

For a fresh seasoning, stack a few leaves, roll them, and chop finely. Start with about 1/2 to 1 teaspoon of chopped fresh leaf per serving stirred into tomato sauce, a marinade, or a vinaigrette. Taste and add a little more only if you want it stronger, since the flavor is bold.

To dry leaves for the pantry, tie small bundles of leafy stems and hang them upside down in a warm, dark, airy spot, or lay the tops in an open paper bag. Leave them 1 to 2 weeks until the leaves are crisp and crumble easily.
Crumble the dried leaves off the stems and store them whole in a labeled airtight jar, away from heat and light.
When cooking with the dried leaf, crush a pinch between your fingers just before adding it to release the aroma, and season sparingly, tasting as you go.
modifiers: For the gentlest result, use only the petals as garnish, since they are much milder than the leaf. Fresh to dried swap: about 1 teaspoon of chopped fresh leaf is roughly 1/3 teaspoon of crumbled dried leaf, so scale down when cooking with the dried pantry version. The florets lose their punch once dried, so enjoy petals fresh and reserve leaves for the drying jar.
preservation: Store fully dried, crumbled leaves whole in a labeled airtight jar kept away from heat and light, and crush just before cooking. Use within about a year for the best aroma. Use fresh petals the same day you pick them, since they fade quickly.
safety: This is the mint family bee balm (Monarda fistulosa), not the citrus bergamot orange (Citrus bergamia) used to flavor Earl Grey tea. The two plants are unrelated, so none of the citrus oil notes or citrus sun sensitivity apply here.

Identify your plant before eating. Wild bergamot has a square stem, paired opposite leaves, a single dense whorl of pale lavender flowers, and a strong oregano or thyme scent when you crush a leaf. The only realistic look-alikes are harmless mint relatives such as mountain mints (Pycnanthemum) and wood mints (Blephilia), but check every feature anyway.

Do not use the look-alike spotted beebalm (Monarda punctata), which shows yellowish, purple-spotted flowers stacked in several tiers and carries a much heavier thymol and carvacrol load. Keep it out of the kitchen.
Use garnish and light seasoning amounts only. The leaf is rich in the aromatic compounds thymol and carvacrol, so add a little, taste as you go, and skip strong or daily leaf teas.
Avoid this leaf during pregnancy. Traditional food guidance keeps it to small culinary garnish amounts and avoids larger or concentrated amounts while pregnant.
If you take prescription medication, keep this to ordinary food amounts and check with your doctor before using larger quantities.
Use only whole fresh or dried leaves and petals. Do not make concentrated, distilled, or essential oil forms.
Prefer your own garden or nursery plants and harvest the leaves. Leave the wild flowering tops for native bees and hummingbird moths, and take only a few florets from cultivated plants.
A map dot is not harvest permission. Gather only from your own garden, or with the landowner's or land manager's permission.
Keep dried leaf, whole plants, and any prepared seasonings out of reach of children and pets.

name: Everlasting Goldenrod: Drying Flowering Tops for Autumn Bundles
teaser: Loose bunches of late summer gold, hung in the dark until the stems snap crisp.
hook: Goldenrod lights up roadsides and old fields across North America from August into October, and its plumes have long been gathered for dried arrangements that hold their color into winter. This card is purely a decorating craft: cut a few flowering tops from an abundant weedy stand, hang them to dry, and use the golden bundles in wreaths and bouquets. One common misconception worth correcting up front: goldenrod is often wrongly blamed for autumn hay fever, but its heavy pollen is carried by insects rather than the wind, and the real wind pollinated culprit is ragweed, which blooms at the same time.

timeline.active: 20 to 30 minutes to cut, strip, and tie
timeline.passive: 1 to 3 weeks of hang drying, longer in humid air

yield: 3 to 5 small bundles, enough for one wreath or a few arrangements

ingredients.note: Cut from a large stand of common weedy goldenrod (Canada or tall goldenrod). Choose heads that are open and freshly gold but not yet gone to fluffy seed, so they hold color and shed less.

ingredients.item: Garden twine, jute string, or rubber bands
ingredients.note: Rubber bands keep gripping as the stems shrink while drying, so bundles do not slip loose.

toolsRequired: A warm, dark, well ventilated space with a hook, line, or nail to hang from
toolsOptional: Airtight container or box for storing finished bundles
steps: Go out on a dry morning after the dew has burned off, and pick a large, dense stand of common weedy goldenrod. Look for plumes that are open and bright gold but have not started turning to fluffy white seed, since those hold their color best and shed the least in the house.
Cut only a small fraction of the stand, taking flowering tops with 12 to 18 inches of stem and leaving most of the heads standing. Snip cleanly with pruners or scissors.
Strip the lower leaves off each stem so they will not mold in the bundle, and shake each stem gently to knock off loose pollen and insects before bringing it indoors.
Gather 5 to 10 stems into a small, loose bunch. Keep bunches small and airy so air moves through the middle, which prevents mold and helps the color set.
Tie the base of the stems tightly with twine or wrap them with a rubber band, then hang each bunch upside down from a hook, line, or nail.

Leave the bundles hanging in a warm, dark, well ventilated room for 1 to 3 weeks, longer in humid climates. Drying in the dark keeps the gold from fading. They are done when the stems snap crisply and the heads feel papery.
Once fully dry, work the bundles into wreaths, swags, or dried bouquets, or simply stand them in a vase.
Store any extra bundles flat or upright in an airtight container away from direct sunlight, where they keep their color for about a year.
modifiers: Mixing in a few stems cut slightly earlier, when the buds are still tight and greenish gold, gives a range of tones in a finished arrangement. For a fuller wreath, dry extra bundles and wire them onto the frame in overlapping layers.
preservation: Fully dried bundles kept in an airtight box away from direct sunlight and damp hold their gold color for roughly a year. Sunlight fades them and humidity can bring back mold, so keep them cool and dark between seasons.
safety: Identify your plant with certainty before cutting. Goldenrod (Solidago) is a genus of many look alike species, and this project is only for common, abundant, weedy kinds such as Canada goldenrod or tall goldenrod.

Do not confuse true goldenrod with rayless goldenrod (Isocoma pluriflora), a different and toxic plant in another genus. True goldenrod has showy yellow ray flowers along plume shaped heads, while rayless goldenrod has small rayless flower clusters and grows mainly in the dry Southwest. If you are unsure of the plant, do not cut it.

Some Solidago species are rare or legally protected, for example Houghton's goldenrod and Short's goldenrod. Harvest only from large stands of the common weedy kinds, and never from solitary, unusual, or shoreline and dune plants, or from any plant you cannot confidently identify as abundant.

Anyone with a ragweed, Asteraceae (also called Compositae), or latex sensitivity, or with asthma, should skip this project or work carefully. Handling the flowers and leaves and breathing in dislodged pollen or fine fluff can cause a skin rash or a breathing reaction in sensitized people. Wear gloves, work in good ventilation, and put on a dust mask when stripping leaves or handling heads near the fluffy seed stage.
Keep finished dried bundles out of reach of pets, livestock, and small children, who might chew on dried plant material.

Map occurrence is not harvest permission. Public land may require permission from the land manager. Cut only a small fraction of any stand and leave most flowering heads standing, since goldenrod is a magnet for native bees and migrating butterflies late in the season.

How To Dry Goldenrod: Harvest, Dry and Store, Farmhouse & Blooms

name: Dandelion Blossom Infused Oil
teaser: An amber oil steeped from dried spring blossoms in a clean jar.
hook: Dandelion flowers turn a jar of plain carrier oil a soft gold after a few weeks in the dark. Steeping petals in oil is one of the oldest folk kitchen and skincare crafts, and this bright spring weed is a forgiving place to start your first infusion. Traditionally the finished oil was a folk body oil smoothed onto hands and elbows after time outdoors. This card covers only the making and its customary external use, and makes no health or benefit claims of any kind.

timeline.active: 20 to 30 min
timeline.passive: 4 to 6 weeks (plus 1 to 3 days drying)

yield: About 1 to 1.5 cups (250 to 375 ml) of strained flower oil from a half-filled pint jar.

ingredients.item: Open dandelion flower heads (Taraxacum officinale), air-dried
ingredients.amount: Enough loose blossoms to half-fill your jar, about 1 to 1.5 cups fresh
ingredients.note: flower heads only, never the root or stems; dry them 1 to 3 days first

ingredients.item: Skin-friendly carrier oil (olive, sunflower, or sweet almond)
ingredients.amount: Enough to cover the blossoms fully, about 1 to 1.5 cups (250 to 375 ml)
ingredients.note: olive keeps longest; sunflower and sweet almond feel lighter and carry less scent

ingredients.amount: Substitute for the fresh blossoms, same jar-half volume
ingredients.note: use these whenever a spray-free harvest site is not certain

toolsRequired: Clean, fully dry glass jar with a lid (half-pint or pint)
Paper or a mesh screen for air-drying the blossoms
Fine strainer plus cheesecloth or a coffee filter
Clean dark glass bottle for storage
toolsOptional: Small saucepan for a warm-water speed infusion
Scissors to snip the heads from the stems
steps: Pick fully open flower heads on a dry day, late morning once the dew has gone, from ground you know has never been sprayed. Snip the yellow head from the stem and leave the root in the ground. Occurrence on the map is not permission, so clear the specific site first.

Spread the blossoms in a single layer on paper or a screen, out of direct sun, for 1 to 3 days until the surface moisture is gone. Fresh, wet flowers make oil that turns cloudy or spoils, so this drying step is the real safety control, not a smell check later.

Half-fill a clean, fully dry jar with the dried blossoms, then pour in carrier oil until the flowers sit under at least half an inch of oil. Slide a clean knife around the inside to release air bubbles and keep every petal submerged.
Slow method: cap the jar, label it, and stand it in a cool dark cabinet for 4 to 6 weeks, giving it a gentle shake whenever you pass by.
Fast method: stand the uncapped jar in a saucepan of water and warm it gently, holding the oil around 100 to 120°F for 2 to 3 hours. Keep it low, never let it simmer, and do not leave it unattended.
Strain the oil through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean dark bottle, pressing the spent flowers to recover the last of it, then discard the petals.
Label the bottle "external use only, do not eat" together with the date, then refrigerate. Store it cool and dark, and discard it if it ever smells rancid.
modifiers: Carrier oil sets the character: olive is the most shelf-stable and traditional, while sunflower and sweet almond feel lighter and lend a fainter scent. Fuller, well-dried blossoms and a longer steep give a deeper gold. The warm-water method trades a little color and shelf life for same-day results. Keep the ratio simple: blossoms loosely filling the jar, with oil covering them completely.
preservation: Because the flowers are dried, the main risks are the oil going rancid and, if any moisture sneaks in, mold or Clostridium botulinum growing in the low-acid oil. Use fully dried blossoms and a clean dry jar, keep the petals submerged, cap to exclude air, refrigerate, and keep the bottle away from light and heat. Dried-flower oils generally keep about three months refrigerated. Discard at the first rancid or off smell, and never taste it to check.
safety: External use only, do not eat. Label the bottle "external use only, do not eat" along with the infusing date so the jar is never mistaken for food, because botanical material steeped in low-acid oil can support Clostridium botulinum and mold if swallowed.

Identify your plant before picking: a true dandelion carries a single flower head on one hollow, unbranched, leafless stem. Skip branched or multi-flowered yellow lookalikes such as cat's ear, hawksbeard, sow thistle, and groundsel or ragwort.

Patch test before wider use: dab a little on the inner forearm and wait about a day. People sensitive to ragweed, chamomile, marigold, chrysanthemum, or feverfew (the Asteraceae or Compositae family) can react to dandelion on the skin. Wash it off and stop using it if any redness, itching, or rash appears.

Use flower heads only, never the root or stems. The latex that most often causes skin reactions concentrates in the root and stems, and the root is a restricted part that needs land-manager permission on public land, so leave it in the ground.

Gather only from ground you know has never been sprayed. Dandelion is the main target of residential broadleaf weed killers (2,4-D, dicamba, mecoprop, weed-and-feed products), so avoid lawns, roadsides, parking-lot edges, and their spray drift. When a spray-free site is not certain, use purchased dried dandelion flowers instead.

Fully air-dry the blossoms and use a clean, dry jar. Cover the flowers completely with oil, cap to keep air out, refrigerate, store away from light and heat, and discard the oil if it ever smells rancid.
Not for broken or infected skin. Keep it out of the eyes, and store it out of reach of children and pets.
Occurrence on the map is not harvest permission. Get permission for the specific site, and remember that many parks and preserves prohibit collecting.
